SEARCH
Package com.falkordb

Interface AsyncGraph

public interface AsyncGraph
An asynchronous view over a Graph: every method mirrors the synchronous Graph operation of the same name but runs it on a caller-supplied Executor and returns a CompletableFuture.

Obtain one with AsyncFalkorDB.wrap(GraphContextGenerator, java.util.concurrent.Executor), backing it with a pool-per-call driver.graph(id) (safe for concurrent calls). The facade owns nothing — neither the wrapped graph nor the executor — so it is not Closeable; the caller drains outstanding futures, shuts the executor down, then closes the graph and driver.

On JDK 21+ this pairs with Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor() to run many concurrent queries cheaply — the executor is supplied by the caller, so this type stays Java-8.

Concurrency & backpressure: real DB concurrency is bounded by the connection pool (poolMaxTotal), not the executor; there is no built-in admission bound, so pair a virtual-thread executor with a finite poolMaxWait (or your own semaphore) if you need one. Cancellation is best-effort: cancelling a returned future frees the caller and skips a not-yet-started query, but cannot interrupt a query already on the wire — bound in-flight work with a per-query/server timeout or a finite socket timeout (not cancel()). Failures surface as the future completing exceptionally (join() throws CompletionException; get() throws ExecutionException). The map/list arguments are read later on the worker thread, so do not mutate them until the future completes.

      CompletableFuture<ResultSet> query(String query)
      Asynchronously runs Graph.query(String).

      Returns:
      a future completing with the result set

      kwargs - the procedure output arguments

      a future completing with the result set

      CompletableFuture<String> copyGraph(String destinationGraphId)
      Asynchronously runs Graph.copyGraph(String).

      Returns:
      a future completing with the copy running-time statistics

    • graph

      GraphContextGenerator graph()
      The wrapped synchronous graph. The caller owns and closes it; use this to reach synchronous or connection-bound operations not exposed asynchronously.
      Returns:
      the wrapped graph
